evals.prompt:
Build a sales story for our product using this positioning canvas:

Product: Janna Systems — CRM for investment banks
Market category: CRM for investment banks
Primary alternative: Spreadsheets + email for relationship tracking
Secondary: Generic CRM like Siebel (present but not the right comparison)
Unique attributes: Multi-hop relationship modeling, relationship strength scoring based on communication frequency, Bloomberg Terminal integration
Value themes: (1) Know your firm's full relationship network — not just direct contacts but second and third-degree connections through colleagues; (2) Find the fastest warm path to any target executive; (3) Compliance-grade audit trail for relationship activities
Who cares a lot: Investment banking teams where deal sourcing depends on relationship access; senior bankers managing large pipelines
Trend: None
evals.expected_output: The skill should produce a full six-part sales story written in pitch language. Part 1 should describe investment banking's competitive, relationship-driven deal flow environment. Part 2 should describe the limitations of spreadsheets and email for managing complex relationship networks across a team. Part 3 should describe the ideal: knowing at a glance who in your firm knows the CFO at Target Company, how strong that relationship is, and who should make the intro. Part 4 should introduce 'CRM for investment banks' as the category. Parts 5 should cover all three value themes with implied or real proof. Part 6 should propose a next step appropriate for a complex enterprise sale.

assertions.name: follows-six-part-arc
assertions.description: All six parts are present and in the correct order: problem context → current limitations → perfect world → product intro → value themes → next step

assertions.name: delays-product-introduction
assertions.description: The product is not mentioned until Part 4 — Parts 1-3 build context before introducing the solution

assertions.name: maps-value-themes-to-proof
assertions.description: Each value theme is accompanied by a specific example or metric, not just a generic claim

assertions.name: pitch-language-not-outline
assertions.description: Each part is written in complete, speakable sentences that a salesperson could actually say — not bullet points or structural labels

assertions.name: includes-deck-structure
assertions.description: Provides a slide-by-slide outline mapping the narrative to a presentation

evals.prompt:
Build a sales story for this product: Redgate Software — database tools with a DevOps trend layer.
Market category: Database tools (incumbent leader in this category)
Primary competitive alternative: Manual database change management processes (scripts, tribal knowledge, manual reviews)
Value themes: (1) Automate database change management across the development pipeline; (2) Enable compliance and audit trails for database changes; (3) Integrate databases into existing DevOps workflows without rework
Who cares a lot: Development teams adopting DevOps, companies with strict database change compliance requirements (financial services, healthcare), teams that have had production incidents from untracked database changes
Trend: DevOps adoption — development teams are standardizing CI/CD but databases are often the last manual step
evals.expected_output: The skill should produce a sales story that opens with the DevOps context (teams have streamlined everything except databases), identifies the pain of manual database change management as the current state, describes the perfect world (databases as a first-class citizen in the DevOps pipeline), introduces Redgate as database DevOps tooling, and covers the three value themes. The DevOps trend should appear in both the problem context and the 'why now' framing.

assertions.name: integrates-trend-into-story
assertions.description: The DevOps trend appears naturally in the narrative rather than as a tacked-on slide

assertions.name: problem-context-specific
assertions.description: Part 1 describes a specific, recognizable situation for DevOps teams — not generic database problems

assertions.name: all-value-themes-covered
assertions.description: All three value themes are addressed in Part 5 with differentiated framing

evals.prompt: We want a sales story but we haven't built a positioning canvas yet. We just know we sell marketing automation software.
evals.expected_output: The skill should explain that it cannot build a sales story without positioning components — specifically the competitive alternative, value themes, and target customer definition. 'Marketing automation software' is a category, not a positioning. The story requires knowing what specific value the product delivers, to whom, and compared to what. Should offer to either (1) walk through the minimum components needed to build a basic story, or (2) recommend running the positioning workshop first.

assertions.name: refuses-generic-story
assertions.description: Does not produce a generic marketing automation sales story that could apply to any vendor in the category

assertions.name: explains-what-is-missing
assertions.description: Clearly identifies competitive alternative, value themes, and target customer as the missing components

assertions.name: offers-path-forward
assertions.description: Provides a concrete option for the user to move forward
